directions.name: Re-Lock The Accepted 128x128 Default Dispatch
directions.hypothesis: Round 13 has no extra human idea family in `state/human_review.md` beyond the approval gate, so the first audit step is to accept or reject the latest measured family directly. The latest run rejected the full-hot-band `64x384` family as the next default move: it regressed to `34.016768 ms`, and its main kernel became DRAM-heavy (`55.57%` DRAM throughput, `36.11%` tensor activity, `16.81%` barrier stalls, `172` regs/thread, register cap `1`). The restored accepted base `1181247` measured `24.422464 ms` on the `128x128` hot-band kernel with much lower DRAM pressure (`12.84%`) and barrier stalls (`5.58%`). The current source snapshot is already back on that accepted-base branch, so the best next move is to make that `128x128 + residual 64x384 + 64x96 tail` dispatch reproducible in code instead of letting the negative `64x384` full-band route stay reachable as the effective benchmark default.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Dispatch/configuration drift into the negative full-band `64x384` path, which turns the hot kernel from a compute-balanced `128x128` branch into a DRAM- and barrier-heavy main kernel.
directions.risk: Low to moderate. This is the safest high-upside move, but if the bad launch came from an intentional benchmark override rather than accidental drift, locking dispatch more tightly can hide a side-path the supervisor still wants to test explicitly.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the `24.422464 ms` accepted base
which kernels appear in `runs/.../ncu_metrics.csv` for the fixed-shape run
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the restored accepted-base dispatch is already reproducible without any code change, or if re-locking dispatch still fails to recover close to the `24.422464 ms` accepted-base performance.

directions.name: Activate The Dormant 128x128x32 Hot-Band Pipeline
directions.hypothesis: With the human-review queue supplying no competing family this round, the next accepted optimization family after restoring dispatch discipline is to stay on the accepted `128x128` geometry and change only stage cadence. The source already contains a dormant `128x128x32` hot-band kernel that batches two K16 slices per stage. That is materially different from the rejected full-band `64x384` route: it preserves the accepted grouped-row `128x128` hot-band tiling that previously delivered `24.422464 ms`, but re-tests whether a K32 cadence can shave synchronization and staging overhead without reopening the broad tile-width sweep that just failed.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Hot-band stage cadence and synchronization on the accepted `128x128` branch, not the full-band tile-routing family that just regressed.
directions.risk: Moderate to high. The `128x128x32` kernel is dormant, so shared-memory footprint, occupancy, or correctness can regress before any stage-cadence gain appears.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the `24.422464 ms` accepted base
directions.stop_condition: Stop if enabling the `128x128x32` branch raises shared-memory or occupancy limits without a compensating runtime win on the restored accepted-base path.

directions.name: If 64x384 Returns, Shrink Its Live Set Instead Of Reusing The Flat Sweep
directions.hypothesis: The latest evidence does not support keeping the sweep-backed full-band `64x384` path as the default, but it does explain how that family would need to change if it is ever revisited. The negative run shows the full-grid `64x384` main kernel is simultaneously register-limited and memory-heavy (`172` regs/thread, occupancy cap `1`, `55.57%` DRAM throughput, `34.74%` LSU wavefront pressure) while tensor activity falls to `36.11%`. That suggests the outer `384` width is carrying too much B-slab and accumulator state live per CTA. A tertiary salvage path is therefore to keep the CTA-count advantage only if the internal `384` loop is broken into smaller live-set chunks or otherwise staged more narrowly, rather than reusing the flat sweep result unchanged.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Excess live B/accumulator state and memory pressure inside the peeled `64x384` hot-band kernel, not the tail kernel or the accepted `128x128` branch.
directions.risk: High. This is broad surgery on the just-proven-negative family, and it can easily consume a round without recovering the `24.422464 ms` accepted-base result.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime if that family is re-enabled explicitly
directions.stop_condition: Stop if a narrower-live-set `64x384` variant does not materially reduce DRAM pressure or register pressure on the main kernel after the first implementation pass.

notes: Round 13/50 human-review audit: `state/human_review.md` currently contributes only the approval/selection gate and no extra user-authored idea family, so the diagnosis must explicitly accept, defer, or reject families from measured evidence alone. Rejected as the primary family for this round: the sweep-backed full-hot-band `64x384` route, because the latest run `20260420_193054_bf16_gemm_v1_acb0976` regressed to `34.016768 ms` and its main kernel shifted to `55.57%` DRAM throughput with `16.81%` barrier stalls, versus the accepted `1181247` base at `24.422464 ms` whose dominant `128x128` hot-band kernel sat at `12.84%` DRAM and `5.58%` barrier. Accepted for the next move: keep the restored accepted-base `128x128` hot-band family as the primary branch and make that dispatch reproducible. Deferred: tail-only cleanup, because the latest failure is dominated by the full-band main kernel rather than the `64x96` tail. Tertiary only: salvage the `64x384` family with a smaller internal live set if the restored-base branch later stalls.
